Q1: My study routine was honestly all over the place this semester. I tried to carve out time in the evenings after my part-time job, but with limited resources at home, I often had to study at the library when I could get there. I'd usually read through materials and take handwritten notes since that's how I learn best. Some weeks I managed about 11 hours, but it was rarely consistent because life kept throwing things at me.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what was happening in my life. I'd have bursts of intense studying right before deadlines, then quieter periods where I could barely look at my coursework. Working to help pay bills meant my schedule was never really my own, so I had to grab study time whenever it appeared rather than sticking to a set routine.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew something was due soon or when I needed to check if there were new announcements. Sometimes the gamified tasks would send me notifications that made me curious enough to log in and see what points or badges I could earn. I also logged in when I felt that anxious knot in my stomach telling me I was falling behind and needed to catch up.
Q4: For a little while, the gamified elements actually made logging in feel more like a habit. I'd check in to see my progress on the leaderboard or complete small tasks for points, which gave me tiny wins that felt good. But then when my attendance dropped and I started feeling overwhelmed, even that routine fell apart. It was easier to maintain when I felt like I was keeping up.
Q5: The reading materials and written guides were most useful to me because I learn best through reading and writing things out. I also appreciated when there were clear summaries or structured notes I could download and annotate. The gamified tasks were surprisingly helpful too because breaking things into smaller pieces made the workload feel less terrifying when my anxiety was high.
Q6: I often skipped the video lectures or only half-watched them because I retain information better through reading. Discussion boards felt awkward to me, and with only having posted four times, I clearly avoided them. I think I was anxious about saying something wrong in front of everyone, and honestly, I didn't always have the mental energy to engage with other people's posts.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to assignments or exams, or when it gave me that sense of accomplishment through the gamified system. If I could read something, take notes, and feel like I actually understood it, that felt valuable. Activities that seemed vague or disconnected from what I'd be graded on felt like a waste of my limited time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. When you're juggling work, limited money, and high anxiety, you have to prioritize ruthlessly. Required activities at least had the pressure of grades behind them, which forced me to engage even when I didn't want to. Optional ones just got pushed aside when things got hard, even if they might have helped me.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded almost everything I could so I could study offline when I didn't have reliable internet or was at work during breaks. I filled notebooks with handwritten summaries and practice problems. I also used free textbook PDFs I found online and YouTube videos to supplement what I didn't understand from the course materials.
Q10: There were many nights where I sat with my printed notes and notebooks for hours, working through concepts, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd reread my handwritten summaries on the bus to work. I'd quiz myself from memory while doing dishes. A lot of my real studying happened completely disconnected from any platform.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download a PDF or document once, then spend days working through it offline. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so it made more sense to have everything saved locally. The LMS would show I accessed something once, but I probably reviewed those same materials five or six times on my own.
Q12: Around midterms, everything crashed down on me at once. My anxiety spiked really badly, and I was working extra shifts because we needed the money. I felt so behind that logging in almost made me panic because I'd see everything I hadn't done. The sadness kind of crept in too, making it hard to find the energy to even try.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or overwhelmed by how much I had to do, the LMS became this source of dread rather than help. I'd log in, see all the incomplete tasks, feel my anxiety rise, and sometimes just log out without doing anything. Competing responsibilities meant the course often had to take a backseat to immediate survival needs.
Q14: There were times I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials, but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd be too anxious or tired to actually absorb anything. I'd watch the minutes tick by on a video without processing a single word. Those sessions probably looked like engagement but were really just me going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed studying for exams even though I knew my exam scores were suffering. The anxiety made it feel like if I started studying and still failed, it would confirm I wasn't smart enough. Avoiding it let me preserve some hope, even though logically I knew that was making things worse. It's a terrible cycle that my resilience eventually helped me push through, but not always in time.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my avoidance. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before something was due, which is probably why my assignment scores were decent even though my overall engagement looked low. The panic of a deadline cut through the anxiety paralysis in a way that regular studying couldn't.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it genuinely lifted my mood and made me want to engage more. It reminded me I could actually do this. But when feedback was delayed or harsh, it fed into my anxiety and made me want to hide from the course. The gamified feedback with points and badges was actually really motivating because it was immediate and felt less personal.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming at times because there was so much content across ten online courses I was managing. When modules were clearly organized with reading materials upfront, I could work with that. But when things felt scattered or when I couldn't tell what was most important, my anxiety made it hard to know where to even start.
Q19: The gamified elements genuinely helped make engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, achievable chunks. But the sheer volume of video content was harder for me as a read-write learner. Also, the discussion requirements felt forced and uncomfortable. Having downloadable materials was crucial for someone in my situation with unreliable internet access.
Q20: I think that could be someone like me who's logging in, looking at everything, maybe starting things but getting overwhelmed before finishing. High anxiety can make you circle around tasks without completing them. Or they might be studying offline and just not submitting because they don't feel ready or confident enough in their work.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My assignment scores were actually decent despite low visible engagement because I was doing a lot of work offline. Someone might download everything once, study extensively on their own, and submit solid work without ever looking 'engaged' by LMS standards. The system can't see notebooks, printed materials, or offline study sessions.
Q22: Course logs can show when you accessed things and what you clicked on, but they miss everything about quality of engagement or offline work. They can't measure understanding, emotional state, or effort. They definitely can't see that I spent three hours with printed notes or that I was too anxious to focus even when I was logged in. They capture presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting in genuine effort to learn, even when it's hard. For me, it's about pushing through anxiety and difficult circumstances to keep trying. It's not just showing up or clicking through things—it's mentally wrestling with concepts until they make sense. Engagement is internal commitment that doesn't always show externally.
Q24: The LMS would never show how hard I fought to stay in this course despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the anxiety attacks before logging in, or the pride I felt when I understood something difficult. It can't capture that I'm a first-generation student with limited resources who's working to survive while studying. My resilience and determination are invisible to any data system.
